What is container stowage planning?

Container stowage planning is the act of allocating space on board a ship for containers loaded at certain port(s) to be discharged at certain port(s), without those containers having to be rehandled at any intermediate (way) port along the route. It is one of the most important ship-operation functions.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 1: What is Stowage Planning (p.1-2)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What inputs are required to perform stowage planning?

Four inputs are required: (1) the scheduled list of ports in rotation order; (2) a summary of containers by size/type/weight and container numbers per load port; (3) a summary of hazardous, reefer and OOG containers per port; and (4) a list of containers already on board for ports beyond the current port.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 2: Required Inputs & Tools (p.2-2)

What is a 'profile' in a bay plan?

A profile is the cross-sectional view of the entire ship covering both deck and under-deck. It shows all stow positions and which containers are discharged at which port, letting the port operations team and stevedores identify the loading sequence and which bays to load/discharge.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 3: Profile (Cross-Sectional View) (p.2-3)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What is the difference between a bayplan and a bay?

A bayplan is the complete cross-sectional view of the whole ship but displayed or printed per bay. A bay is one of the compartments the vessel is split into, numbered 01 (toward the bow) up to as high as 88 (toward the stern) depending on ship size.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 4: Bayplan and Bay (p.3-3)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What do odd and even bay numbers indicate?

Odd-numbered bays (1, 3, 5...) indicate a 20' container stow, while even-numbered bays (2, 4, 6...) indicate a 40' container stow. Each small square block in the plan represents one 20' unit space.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 5: Bay Numbering: 20' vs 40' Stows (p.4-4)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
How are rows numbered on a container vessel?

Row is the athwartship position across the ship's width. Numbering starts at 01 in the center and progresses outward, with odd numbers to starboard (right) and even numbers to port (left).

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 6: Row (Athwartship Position) (p.5-5)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What is a tier, and what do hatch covers separate?

A tier denotes the vertical level at which a container is stacked. Hatch covers are the covers separating the deck from the under-deck: the area above the hatch-cover line is the deck (visible from outside), and the area below is the under-deck (not visible).

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 7: Tier and Hatch Covers; Deck vs Under-Deck (p.6-6)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
In what order is space allocated to containers?

Space is allocated firstly by destination - the farthest discharge port at the bottom and the next port of call on top - and secondly by weight, with the heaviest boxes at the bottom and the lightest on top.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 8: Stowage Allocation Rules: Destination & Weight Order (p.6-6)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
Can two 20' containers be stowed on top of a 40' container?

For lashing and securing reasons, a 40' container may sit on top of two 20' containers, but two 20' containers may not sit on top of a 40' container - unless it is under deck and surrounded by other containers or within cell guides.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 9: 40'/20' Stacking & Lashing Rule (p.6-6)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What is the port rotation and how does it affect stacking order?

The vessel rotation (from Durban) is Felixstowe, Antwerp, Le Havre, Hamburg, Rotterdam. Since Felixstowe is the first port of call, Felixstowe containers are stacked on top, then Antwerp, Le Havre, Hamburg, and Rotterdam at the bottom because Rotterdam is the last call. Letter codes: F=Felixstowe, A=Antwerp, Ae=Antwerp Empty, H=Hamburg, L=Le Havre, R=Rotterdam, X=a 40' container.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 10: Destination Color/Letter Codes & Port Rotation (p.7-7)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
How do you read the stow position 110910?

Stow position 110910 decodes as Bay 11, Row 09, Tier 10. A six-digit stow position is read as Bay (2 digits) + Row (2 digits) + Tier (2 digits), allowing any container's location to be identified from the bay plan.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 11: Stow Position Reading (Bay-Row-Tier) (p.7-10)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What is a restow and why is it costly?

A restow is offloading a container from the ship and placing it back, at the same or a different stow position - often caused by incorrect stowage or a late change of destination. It is costly because reaching one wrongly stowed box can require restowing all overstowing containers (e.g., 12x40' for Antwerp plus opening the hatch cover and restowing a 20' for Le Havre) before the target container can be discharged.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 12: Restow: Definition, Cause & Cost (p.8-8)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What is deck stress / tier weight and how does it relate to stability?

Deck stress (tier weight) is the maximum weight each tier/row can carry per the ship's design. For example, four 26-ton containers in one tier may not be loadable because the concentrated heavy weight can affect stability, whereas five tiers of empties may be acceptable. Planning software flags violations as errors.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 13: Deck Stress / Tier Weight & Stability (p.9-9)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
What software and file format are used for ship stowage planning?

Commonly used ship-planning software includes CASP, MACS3 and Bulko. They use the BAPLIE file format, which is structured according to the UN/EDIFACT standard.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 14: Planning Software & BAPLIE/EDIFACT (p.9-9)
You are PortSLM, a container-terminal domain assistant. You generate grounded explanations only and never make operational decisions. Every answer must cite its source.
Why is correct stowage planning important for safety?

Correct stowage planning and proper lashing/securing of containers are critical to prevent stack collapses on board and containers lost at sea, helping avoid maritime disasters. Much stowage is now done in centralised hubs.

[출처] The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 15: Safety Importance & Centralised Hubs (p.9-9)

Provide a grounded explanation for the topic 'Bay Numbering: 20' vs 40' Stows' in stowage planning.

Per The Basics of Container Stowage Planning, Bay Numbering: 20' vs 40' Stows: Odd numbered bays (1, 3, 5 etc) mean that it is a 20' container stow and even numbered bays (2, 4, 6 etc) mean that it is a 40' container stow. Bay 09/11 (10) and Bay 13/15 (14) are used as an example. What you are seeing is the cross-section of the ship both on deck and under deck. Each of the small square blocks represents a 20' unit space.

Provide a grounded explanation for the topic 'Restow: Definition, Cause & Cost' in stowage planning.

Per The Basics of Container Stowage Planning, Restow: Definition, Cause & Cost: A restow is a move where a container is offloaded from on board the ship and put back onto the ship either at the same stow position or a different stow position. This could be due to incorrect stowage of a container - say loaded wrongly for Le Havre instead of Felixstowe - or a change of destination requested at a later stage. Provide a grounded explanation for the topic 'Deck Stress / Tier Weight & Stability' in stowage planning.

Per The Basics of Container Stowage Planning, Deck Stress / Tier Weight & Stability: Each of the bays has a deck stress or tier weight which is the maximum allowed weight that each tier/row can carry as per the design of the ship. For example, if there are about 4 containers in a tier each weighing 26 tons, it may not be possible to accommodate all 4 in one tier as this might affect stability due to the heavy nature of the cargo. However, if there are 5 tiers of empty containers as shown in Bay 15, it might be possible to load. These calculations are performed by the computer an
